shared_properties: Overall footprint stays 144x16px so it drops into the same floor sockets as the slick strip.
Top collision edge remains flat and aligned to the 16px tile cadence used across the shaft.
Stone understructure keeps the same deep-slate shadow band used by the base floor strip.
differences: Mid-tone moss clusters replace the icy highlight band and push the dominant surface hue to #4E5940.
Grip read becomes denser through extra edge noise and short vertical root pixels.
Interior breakup shifts from glossy streaks to mottled organic patches to communicate medium friction.

context.usage: Mid-friction floor used to reset player control after high-speed descent segments.

shape.silhouette: Flat floor strip with moss tufts and stone edge chips.
shape.collision_read: Top edge remains clean while the interior pixels communicate added grip.

acceptance_criteria: Moss clusters never break the top collision silhouette of the floor strip.
The strip reads more grippy than the slick floor through warmer green-grey coverage and reduced specular pixels.

reference_images.label: slip_down_moodboard
reference_images.purpose: Grounds stone texture breakup, low-key lighting, and vertical shaft composition for the active environment asset.

bevy_constraints.bundle_strategy: Tilemap-backed strip or repeated SpriteBundle instances aligned to medium-friction lanes.
bevy_constraints.asset_loading: Load during the Loading state and swap into shaft chunks where the gameplay layer assigns 0.5 friction.
runtime_notes: Use Bevy 0.13 with ImagePlugin::default_nearest() so every asset stays pixel-perfect at 4x upscale.
Keep this strip on the same tile grid as the slick floor so route variants can reuse level sockets.
Use palette separation rather than outline thickness to distinguish it from the low-friction strip.
